name: Typonium
classification_notes: Built on simple geometric forms with circular bowls, drawing from Bauhaus and universal design principles. The soft roundness and minimalist construction firmly place it in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans in eight weights with reverse ink-traps at stem-bowl junctions, creating a tension between soft rounded forms and sharply defined contours.
description: Built on circular bowls and minimal forms referencing Bauhaus and universal design, the family uses reverse ink-traps where stems meet bowls to introduce visual tension against otherwise warm, rounded strokes. Ascenders and descenders carry the same gentle roundness throughout, keeping the overall tone approachable. Eight weights each with a matching italic give it a broad range across body text and display settings.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Typonium as fundamentally grounded in simple geometric forms and modernist aesthetics, citing Bauhaus and universal design as its foundation. They highlight the reverse ink-traps at stem-bowl junctions as its most distinctive feature, creating sharp visual tension against the soft, rounded overall character. The family is framed as a quiet contradiction held in balance, like two opposing states coexisting simultaneously.

name: Rollsign Gothic
classification_notes: A tall, narrow display typeface derived from hand-drawn transit rollsigns, combining utilitarian signage lettering with strong vertical proportions suited to poster and headline use.
summary: A tall, narrow display gothic drawn from the hand-lettered rollsigns of pre-digital bus and train signage.
description: The proportions are dictated by the original problem of rollsign lettering: fitting a large amount of text into a tight rectangular space without sacrificing legibility. Seven weights with matching italics give the family range from restrained to commanding, and the retained handcrafted quality keeps it from feeling purely mechanical. Suited to posters, video titles and packaging where vertical compression and visual weight are doing the work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe the concept as a decade in the making, inspired by the hand-drawn rollsigns once used on buses and trains. The tall, narrow rectangular proportion reflects the demands of that original signage format, while the design preserves a utilitarian, handcrafted character alongside clarity and strength. Though rollsigns are no longer in use, the family is positioned for contemporary applications including posters, video titles and product packaging across seven weights, each with a matching italic, supporting a wide range of Latin-based languages.

name: Quic
classification_notes: Built on geometric forms but with humanist inflections in characters like 'a', 'b' and 'g' that reference cursive and brushwork, placing it squarely in the geometric sans genre with a hybrid character.
summary: A geometric sans with cursive-influenced letterforms in characters like 'a', 'b' and 'g', offering eight weights with matching italics and broad Latin coverage.
description: The construction is geometric at its core, but individual characters draw from fluid, rapid strokes associated with cursive handwriting and expressive brushwork. This gives the family a lightness and clarity that sets it apart from more rigidly constructed geometric sans designs. Eight weights with matching italics make it versatile for editorial and identity work where a touch of personality is wanted without sacrificing legibility.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Quic as primarily built on simple geometric forms, with its distinguishing feature being the cursive and brushwork-inspired treatment of characters such as 'a', 'b' and 'g'. They note that while typefaces with similar qualities have appeared since the 2000s, few match this level of refinement or commitment to a singular design vision. The family offers eight weights with matching italics and broad Latin-based language support.

name: Minimalized
classification_notes: The typeface draws on the modular, grid-based aesthetic of 1990s digital design, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with an industrial, reductive character.
summary: A geometric sans built as an homage to the grid-driven, ornament-free aesthetic of 1990s digital and internet design.
description: Eight weights with matching italics, each referencing the artificial, modular sensibility that defined early computer-era graphic design. The designer frames it as a personal tribute to the period in which he first studied design, capturing the stripped-back neutrality that emerged from widespread adoption of computers. Broad Latin language support makes it practical alongside its nostalgic premise.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Minimalized as an homage to the early days of computers and the internet, particularly the 1990s atmosphere of grid systems, modular structures and an industrial aesthetic that rejected traditional ornamentation. The family comprises eight weights each with a corresponding italic, and supports a wide range of Latin-based languages.

name: Higgs
classification_notes: A narrow, square-shaped display typeface blending sci-fi aesthetics with 19th-century wood type sensibility; the reverse ink trap treatment at stem-bowl junctions makes it too stylised for body text, firmly placing it in the display category.
summary: A narrow, square-cut display family with reverse ink traps at stem-bowl junctions, drawing on both sci-fi aesthetics and 19th-century wood poster types.
description: Seven weights with matching italics make up this display family, its character defined by a reverse ink trap treatment where stems meet bowls, producing a sharp, slightly notched quality unusual among contemporary typefaces. The narrow, upright construction recalls the condensed wood types of Victorian poster printing, while the overall squareness edges it towards a science-fiction visual vocabulary. Latin-based language support is broad, making it usable across Western European markets.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Higgs as a somewhat square-shaped and narrow typeface drawing on the atmosphere of sci-fi films and anime, while also taking inspiration from the wood typefaces of 19th-century poster printing. Its most distinctive trait is a reverse ink trap treatment at the junctions of stems and bowls, giving it a sharp, striking and stylish character. The family consists of seven weights, each with an italic, and supports a wide range of Latin-based languages.

name: Poipoi
classification_notes: A bold display sans with rounded terminals designed as a layered system for 3D effects, clearly falling into the display category with its poster-oriented, high-impact construction rooted in wood type tradition.
summary: A layered 3D display sans with rounded terminals, built on the wood type tradition of stacking separate printing plates to create depth through colour.
description: Each of the three styles, Standard, Highlight and Shadow, functions as a discrete layer that can be stacked in design software to produce a full three-dimensional effect. The rounded terminals give the letterforms a casual warmth that sits comfortably in playful branding and poster work. All three layers can also be used independently, and the family covers an extensive range of Latin character sets.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Poipoi as a bold 3D sans-serif display family designed for posters, logos and high-impact headlines. They frame it as a digital reimagining of the classic wood type stacking system, where separate printing plates were inked in different colours to create multi-colour visuals, with each layer acting like a printing plate for assigning colour and building depth.

name: Debugger
classification_notes: A monospaced family with octagonal and diagonal construction, designed explicitly for coding and programming contexts, with mechanically slanted rather than cursive italics.
summary: A monospaced family with octagonal construction designed for coding and programming environments, with particular attention to disambiguating commonly confused characters.
description: Built around an octagonal and diagonal letterform construction that gives it a distinct sci-fi quality, it addresses the practical legibility demands of code editors by sharpening distinctions between zero and O, one and I, and two and Z. The italic styles are mechanically slanted rather than cursive, staying consistent with the upright roman structure to avoid disrupting reading flow in programming contexts. Latin-based language support makes it broadly applicable across international development and tech-forward design projects.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Debugger as a futuristic, scientific and digital-inspired monospaced family built for development, programming, coding and data presentation. They highlight its octagonal and diagonal construction as the defining visual characteristic, and emphasise its functional clarity through careful disambiguation of commonly confused character pairs. Italic styles are intentionally mechanically slanted rather than cursive, as Dharma note that cursive styles are not suited to programming contexts.

name: Tokyo Olive
classification_notes: A geometric, stencil-jointed display typeface with open-style layering, designed explicitly for decorative and expressive use; the voluminous, postmodern structure and layered colour system place it firmly in display territory.
summary: A layered display typeface drawing on the postmodern, fanciful typography of late-1980s Japanese advertising.
description: Inspired by a brief wave of decorative postmodernism in 1980s Japan, the typeface pairs a voluminous geometric structure with rounded stencil joints and a classic open style. Five styles function as stackable printing plates, allowing separate colours to be assigned to each layer for multi-colour compositions. Suited to posters, packaging and branding where layered typographic play is the intent.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Tokyo Olive as a nostalgic homage to the playful, girlish aesthetics of mid-to-late 1980s Japanese advertising. The family of five styles is built for layered colour typography, with each style acting like a woodtype plate so that users can stack layers and freely assign different colours to create open-style effects and vibrant compositions.

name: Senpai Coder
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a monospaced coding font with serif detailing drawn from typewriter conventions, making it a serifed mono rather than a clean geometric or grotesque mono.
summary: A typewriter-inspired monospaced family from Dharma Type built for coding, with serif detailing and a slanted-Roman italic rather than cursive forms.
description: Designed specifically for development and programming environments, the family addresses common legibility pitfalls by carefully distinguishing zero from uppercase O, one from uppercase I, and two from Z. Italic styles use a pronounced slant at a lighter weight than the uprights for improved contrast, avoiding cursive alternates that can disrupt readability in code editors. Serif detailing throughout gives the family a typewriter character without sacrificing the clarity that long coding sessions demand.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Senpai Coder as a typewriter-inspired monospaced font family built for development, programming, coding and tabular layouts. They highlight three priorities: easy to distinguish, easy to identify and easy to read, achieved through carefully refined letterforms that separate commonly confused characters. The italic styles feature a pronounced slant and slightly lighter weight than the uprights, using a slanted Roman rather than cursive approach. Serif detailing adds a typewriter personality while broad Latin language support makes it suitable for global developers and designers.

name: Madromit
classification_notes: A geometric, grid-based display face with rounded corners and oval bowls referencing early digital aesthetics; the layered inline styles draw from engraving traditions, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A layered display typeface that fuses 1980s–90s primitive digital aesthetics with classic engraving inline styles, built for colour-stacking workflows.
description: Constructed on a strict grid with rounded corners, oval bowls and right-angled joints, the design draws directly from early computer advertisement typography. The family ships in five styles conceived as printing plates: a Standard base layer and Fill variants that can be stacked in Photoshop or Illustrator to produce open inline effects in any colour combination. The result sits somewhere between retro pixel-era lettering and decorative engraved display type.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Madromit as a nostalgic display typeface inspired by 1980s–90s computer advertisements, a period they characterise as Primitive Digital Design. The family blends that grid-based geometric construction with classic engraving Open Style techniques, and is built around five layerable styles intended for colour-stacking effects in layer-based design software.

name: Best Choice
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a monospaced typeface for programming and coding environments, with deliberate disambiguation of commonly confused characters and slanted-roman italics rather than cursive forms.
summary: A monospaced family from Dharma Type built for coding and programming, with character disambiguation and slanted-roman italics prioritised over cursive forms.
description: Designed around the three qualities Dharma Type identify as central to a good coding font: distinguishability, identifiability and readability. Characters frequently confused in technical contexts, such as zero and uppercase O or one and uppercase I, have been drawn to minimise ambiguity. The italic styles use a slanted roman rather than cursive forms, and are cut slightly lighter than the uprights to reinforce the distinction between the two.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Best Choice as a next-generation monospaced family built for development, programming and tabular layouts. They highlight its careful attention to commonly confused character pairs and its deliberate use of slanted-roman italics, which are also slightly lighter in weight, to support clarity in coding contexts. The family covers Latin-based scripts including Vietnamese and Cyrillic.

name: Golden Decades
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric, minimal sans-serif rooted in Swiss modernism and the functionalist traditions of the Industrial Revolution through the Swiss Style period.
summary: A geometric sans with rounded bowls and open counters, drawing on Swiss modernist principles across eight weights with matching italics.
description: Built on the functionalist legacy of Swiss modernism, this geometric sans prioritises clarity and neutrality without drifting into sterility. Rounded bowls and open counters give it a measured approachability that works across editorial layouts, digital interfaces and branding. Language support covers Latin Extended and basic Cyrillic, with a stylistic alternate for the lowercase 'a' available via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Golden Decades as rooted in the principles that shaped modern typography, tracing a lineage from the Industrial Revolution through the Swiss Style to the present day. They frame the typeface as a revival of that modernist legacy with a contemporary sensibility, offering cool, calm neutrality through rounded bowls and open counters, with eight weights, matching italics and extensive Latin and Cyrillic language support.

name: Short Films
classification_notes: Art Deco-inspired with geometric construction, open counters, rounded bowls and stroke contrast. Decorative enough for display but positioned as readable for body text, placing it firmly in the display category with art deco subcategory.
summary: An Art Deco-influenced display family with open counters, rounded bowls and high stroke contrast, available in six weights with matching italics.
description: Draws on Art Deco geometry while softening it with rounded bowls and wide-open counters, producing a rhythm the foundry describes as simultaneously pop and elegant. Six weights from Thin to Black each carry matching italics, giving the family range across titles, logos and body text. Latin coverage extends to Western, Central and South-Eastern European scripts alongside basic Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Short Films as a fresh and distinctive family inspired by Art Deco, blending retro and futuristic qualities with geometric and humanistic traits. They highlight its open counters, rounded bowls and stroke contrast as giving it a dual character — decorative yet readable — making it suited to both headline and body text use across print and digital platforms.

name: Tamba Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as geometric sans-serif with a condensed, squarish structure; strong geometric construction is the defining characteristic rather than humanist or grotesque traits.
summary: A condensed geometric sans in seven weights with a squarish structure, designed to hold attention without sacrificing legibility at large sizes.
description: Built around a bold, slightly squarish geometry, Tamba Sans sits somewhere between a display grotesque and a pure geometric sans. Seven weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, giving the family enough range for digital interfaces, branding and editorial work. Latin Extended and basic Cyrillic coverage broadens its reach across European markets.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Tamba Sans as a bold, geometric sans-serif with a condensed and slightly squarish structure that balances eye-catching style with functional readability. They position it as a response to the challenge of standing out in a content-saturated digital world, noting its seven weights with matching italics and broad Latin and Cyrillic script support across international platforms.

name: City Boys
classification_notes: Described as leaning slightly humanist with vertical stress and careful contrast calibration, placing it at the intersection of humanist sans and rational sans traditions.
summary: A contrasted sans-serif with a humanist lean and vertical stress, offered in seven weights with matching italics for screen and print work.
description: Built on a well-balanced skeleton, City Boys combines vertical stress with a modest contrast ratio intended to hold up at small sizes across both screen and print. Seven weights run from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, giving the family enough range for headings, body text and branding. A companion subfamily, City Boys Soft, offers the same structure with rounded corners for a softer register.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe City Boys as a fashionable, contrasted sans-serif designed for modern versatility, with clean structure and stylish contrast suited to web, digital, print and merchandise. They note that the letterforms are basic, natural and neutral, built on a well-balanced skeleton, and that the contrast ratio has been carefully calibrated for clarity and elegance at small sizes. The family covers a broad range of Latin and basic Cyrillic languages, with compatibility across Mac Roman, Windows-1252 and Adobe Latin 1–3 encodings.

name: City Boys Soft
classification_notes: Described as having a humanist structure with vertical stress and moderate contrast, placing it between humanist and transitional sans categories; the soft corners and refined letterforms distinguish it from strict geometric or grotesque classifications.
summary: A contrasted sans with softened corners and vertical stress, offered in seven weights with italics for both screen and print use.
description: Built on a clean skeleton with humanist proportions but vertical stress, City Boys Soft sits between warmth and modernity without committing fully to either. The contrast ratio is tuned for contemporary applications, from digital interfaces to branded print, and the full family spans seven weights each paired with a matching italic. A companion family, City Boys, offers identical structure with standard sharp corners for a crisper alternative.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe City Boys Soft as a fashionable, contrasted sans-serif designed for versatility across web, digital, print and branded merchandise. Its letterforms are natural and neutral, with a somewhat humanist structure and vertical stress that gives the typeface a modern, refined character. The softened corners distinguish it from its companion, City Boys, which retains standard sharp corners for a crisper appearance.

name: Mid Century Sans
classification_notes: Built on highly geometric letterforms with Bauhaus influence, but with humanist softening drawn from Mid-Century Modern aesthetics, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category with organic undertones.
summary: A geometric sans with eight weights and matching italics, blending Bauhaus clarity with the softer, more approachable warmth of Mid-Century Modern design.
description: The family takes its cues from early twentieth-century modernist typography whilst tempering the rigidity of pure geometric construction with friendlier, more organic stroke details. Eight weights run from Thin to ExtraBold with matching italics, and language support covers Latin Extended and basic Cyrillic scripts. A stylistic alternate for the lowercase 'a' is available via OpenType, allowing a switch between the geometric single-storey and a more conventional double-storey form.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Mid Century Sans as built on highly geometric shapes, citing the Bauhaus principle of absolute clarity in typography. They distinguish it from strict modernism by drawing on Mid-Century Modern design, an American softening of the International and Bauhaus styles, resulting in letterforms that are clean and functional yet friendly and human. The family supports a wide range of Latin and basic Cyrillic languages with broad encoding support.

name: Slow Tempo
classification_notes: Low contrast letterforms with geometric construction and humanistic terminals place this firmly in the humanist sans category, with open apertures and a clean skeletal structure typical of the style.
summary: A low-contrast humanist sans with geometric construction, open apertures and eight weights with matching italics for body text and display use.
description: Gentle curves at stem intersections and large open apertures give the typeface a calm, airy quality that sets it apart from strictly geometric neutrals. The large x-height and humanistic terminals support legibility across a range of sizes, from running text to headings. Language coverage spans Latin-based scripts and basic Cyrillic, making it broadly usable across international projects.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Slow Tempo as a relaxed, loose-fit font with natural and neutral letterforms built on a clean skeletal structure. They highlight its gentle curvature at stem intersections, large open apertures, low contrast and geometric construction, complemented by a large x-height and humanistic terminals. The family ships in eight weights with matching italics and covers a wide range of Latin-based languages plus basic Cyrillic.

name: Bebas Neue Pro
classification_notes: A tall, condensed all-caps display sans with geometric construction; the Pro version adds lowercase and italics but retains the bold, headline-oriented character of the original.
summary: An expanded professional edition of the widely-used Bebas Neue, now with lowercase letters, true italics and three width variants.
description: Bebas Neue Pro builds on the original 2010 all-caps family that became ubiquitous in headline and logo work across a decade of global design. The new release adds lowercase characters drawn to match the uppercase, fully redrawn italics for every weight, and three widths — Normal, SemiExpanded and Expanded — considerably broadening its range of use. Tabular figures via OpenType and near-complete European Latin plus selected Cyrillic round out a release aimed squarely at professional production.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Bebas Neue Pro as a premium professional upgrade to one of the most widely used all-caps font families in the world, originally released in 2010. The new version was quietly in development for years and introduces lowercase letters designed to match the original uppercase style, refined uppercase characters across the weight range, fully redrawn italics, tabular figures, three widths, and support for almost all European languages alongside selected Cyrillic characters.

name: Baby Baby
classification_notes: A purely decorative, experimental display family built on a striped mono-line construction with no text utility; designed explicitly for layering and colour effects, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A striped, mono-line display family built for layering, with decomposed fill styles that stack to produce colour gradients and rim effects.
description: Constructed entirely from straight mono-lines with no calligraphic influence, it achieves a cold, mechanical aesthetic suited to titles, logos and high-impact graphics. The family splits into four sub-families: Upright and Slanted for immediate impact, Fill for five expanding stem-width layers, Open for additional stacking options, and Color for pre-built gradient and stripe combinations. The layering system allows each style to be stacked with independent colour assignments, producing typographic effects that go well beyond a single typeface.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Baby Baby as a super-experimental display font family built with straight mono-lines throughout, even in curves and connections, to achieve a flat, dry, mechanical and futuristic look. The Fill sub-family decomposes the striped forms into five mono-line layers of increasing stem width, while the Open and Color sub-families extend the stacking system further, with the Color family offering pre-made examples including coloured stripes, gradients and rim effects for limitless layering combinations.

name: Rama Gothic Rounded
classification_notes: A condensed sans-serif display face drawn from 19th-century wood type tradition, with rounded corners added for a softened retro aesthetic; sits firmly in the display category given its poster-oriented proportions and decorative intent.
summary: A condensed, rounded sans-serif drawn from 19th-century wood type, softened with rounded corners for poster and title work.
description: Built on the proportions of vintage American wood type, this face pairs tight condensed spacing with rounded corners to give bold display settings a warmer, more approachable quality. It ships alongside a free Gothic Extras set of borders, ornaments and frames drawn from Hamilton type catalogues, extending its utility for retro-themed layouts. A non-rounded sibling, Rama Gothic, and a slab companion, Rama Slab, complete the broader family.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Rama Gothic Rounded as an antiqued sans-serif inspired by 19th-century wood type, designed to evoke retro charm and nostalgia. Its condensed proportions and rounded details suit posters, titles and vintage-flavoured designs, while every glyph is crafted to capture 1800s typography refreshed with a contemporary softness. A free Gothic Extras set of borders, ornaments and frames inspired by Hamilton's vintage type catalogues is included.

name: Dharma Gothic Rounded
classification_notes: A condensed display sans with rounded terminals and an antiqued character, drawing from 19th-century wood type traditions. Its bold proportions and retro warmth place it firmly in the display category rather than a general-purpose grotesque.
summary: A condensed display sans with rounded terminals drawing from 19th-century wood type, offering 42 styles for poster and titling work.
description: Built around the proportions of Victorian wood type, this family softens the harder edges of the original Dharma Gothic with rounded terminals that give it a warmer, more approachable character. Forty-two styles make it a broad workhorse for display settings, with OpenType alternates for g, r and y, tabular figures for data layouts and a companion Gothic Extras set of borders and ornaments lifted from Hamilton's 1800s catalogues. Best suited to posters, packaging and titles where a retro vernacular tone is wanted without full distress.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Dharma Gothic Rounded as an antiqued sans serif inspired by 19th-century wood type, given a softer and friendlier character through rounded terminals. The family spans 42 styles and is positioned for posters, titles and bold visual applications. A free Gothic Extras set of borders, ornaments and frames from Hamilton's vintage catalogues accompanies the release, and the family sits alongside related offerings including Dharma Gothic, Dharma Slab and the distressed Dharma Gothic P.

name: Dr Slab
classification_notes: A display slab serif designed specifically for layered 3D effects, with separate styles (Base, Rim, Shadow, Ext) functioning as printing plates. Rounded slab serifs and bold proportions place it firmly in display territory.
summary: A bold, rounded slab serif built around a layered printing-plate system for creating 3D display type.
description: Styled after the multi-pass colour printing traditions of wood type and engraved print, Dr Slab separates its design into discrete styles (Base, Rim, Shadow and Ext) that stack in layers within Photoshop, Illustrator or similar apps. Each layer takes its own colour, replicating the effect of overlapping printing plates. The Base style also stands alone as a clean, rounded slab for friendly branding or casual poster work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type position Dr Slab as a 3D serif family for posters, logos and display typography, drawing on the layered colour composition traditions of wood type and early printing. The system uses stackable font styles as digital printing plates, with the Base style functioning independently as a bold, rounded slab with casual charm.

name: Victorian Orchid
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as blending transitional, modern and slab influences, with humanistic warmth, vertical stress and low-contrast sturdy serifs. The overall disposition leans transitional, with its mixed heritage kept as a notable detail.
summary: A five-weight serif family drawing on Victorian decorative lettering, blending transitional, modern and slab traits into a single, readable design.
description: Designed for both text and display use, the family takes its most distinctive cues from Victorian decorative type: unusual serif treatments on diagonal forms like the A, and a dramatic double-storey lowercase g. Five weights from Light to Bold each include true italics and around 500 glyphs with full OpenType support. The design sits at an unusual crossroads of transitional warmth, modern vertical stress and slab sturdiness, giving it a range uncommon in historically-inflected serifs.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Victorian Orchid as a beautiful, organic serif family for text and display, combining the elegance of the Victorian era with contemporary typographic versatility. They highlight its distinctive serifs on diagonal letterforms, its dramatic lowercase g rooted in historical type, and its position at the intersection of transitional, modern and slab serif traditions. The family ships with five weights, true italics, approximately 500 glyphs per style and comprehensive OpenType features.

name: Code Saver
classification_notes: Explicitly designed for coding and tabular layouts with a fixed-pitch design; the condensed width and programming-environment focus place it firmly in the monospaced category.
summary: A condensed monospaced family from Dharma Type built for code editors and tabular data, available in three weights with matching 11-degree italics.
description: Drawn at 93.33% of standard width, it fits more characters per line than a typical monospaced font without sacrificing legibility in numerals and punctuation. The six styles cover Regular, Medium and Bold with paired italics, available in both OpenType and TrueType formats. The aesthetic is clean and unobtrusive, aimed at developers who want something fresher than the usual coding font staples.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Code Saver as a next-generation monospaced font family designed for programming environments and data display, combining clarity, efficiency and a contemporary design sensibility. They highlight its 93.33% condensed width for maximising screen space, its strong character distinction especially in numerals, and its availability across six styles in OpenType and TrueType formats.

name: Vincente
classification_notes: Explicitly Didone-inspired with high contrast between thick and thin strokes and condensed proportions; the vintage ironwork and botanical illustration references align with 19th-century Didone display traditions.
summary: A condensed Didone serif in six weights, drawing on vintage ironwork and antique botanical illustration for its formal warmth.
description: Built on high-contrast Didone proportions with condensed letterforms, Vincente brings a degree of handcrafted character through details like the curved tail of the 'y'. The six-weight family covers Light through ExtraBold and supports a broad range of Latin-based languages. Suited to editorial layouts, branding and display settings where a refined but slightly antique personality is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Vincente as a contemporary serif typeface with Didone-inspired elegance and condensed proportions, drawing from vintage ironwork and antique botanical illustration books. The letterforms are described as simple and orthodox yet enriched with subtle distinctive accents, with sophisticated curves that carry handcrafted warmth. The family spans six weights and supports almost all Latin-based languages, positioned for editorial design, branding and elegant display typography.

name: Bebas Neue Rounded
classification_notes: A display-weight all-caps geometric sans derived from Bebas Neue, distinguished by fully rounded terminals and corners that soften its otherwise rigid geometric structure.
summary: A rounded variant of Bebas Neue with softened terminals and corners, aimed at branding and packaging where the original's sharpness is too severe.
description: Built on the same core proportions as Bebas Neue, this version replaces hard corners and terminals with fully rounded forms to introduce a warmer, more approachable register. Dharma Type position it as softer than the intermediate Bebas Neue SemiRounded, making it the gentlest expression within the Bebas family. Suited to packaging, display headlines and brand identities that need the typeface's clean impact without its harder edge.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Bebas Neue Rounded as a softened reinterpretation of Bebas Neue, featuring rounded corners and terminals for a warmer and friendlier appearance. While it shares the same core structure and proportions as the original, it offers a gentler tone than Bebas Neue SemiRounded, making it suitable for branding, packaging and display work. They note it is available at an affordable price and expands the expressive versatility of the Bebas family while staying true to its clean, impactful roots.

name: Bebas Neue SemiRounded
classification_notes: An all-caps geometric display face derived from Bebas Neue, with rounded corners softening the otherwise hard geometric structure. Categorised as display due to its tall, narrow proportions and headline-first orientation.
summary: A semi-rounded variant of Bebas Neue that softens the original's sharp corners without sacrificing its tall, condensed structure.
description: Sitting between the original Bebas Neue and the fully rounded variant, this cut applies subtle corner rounding to introduce a warmer tone while keeping the same proportions intact. The result is an all-caps display face with enough rigidity for bold headlines and enough softness for contemporary branding work. It fills a specific niche within the Bebas family for designers who find the original too hard-edged but the fully rounded version too soft.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Bebas Neue SemiRounded as a softened variation of the popular Bebas Neue, featuring subtly rounded corners for a warmer, more natural look. Positioned between the original and the fully rounded Bebas Neue Rounded, it is presented as a balance of strength and softness suited to modern branding, headlines and display typography.

name: Fairweather
classification_notes: Fairweather is a condensed sans-serif with neutral, plain letterforms designed specifically around condensed proportions for legibility in tight settings, placing it firmly in the condensed grotesque/sans tradition.
summary: A nine-weight condensed sans-serif with matching italics, built for tight layouts where legibility and neutrality matter.
description: Fairweather is a condensed sans-serif with plain, unobtrusive letterforms suited to titles, captions and body text in space-constrained settings. Nine weights run from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and the family covers most Latin-based languages. Its restrained character means it sits quietly behind the content rather than drawing attention to itself.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Fairweather as clear, transparent and lucid, reminiscent of a crisp spring or autumn sky. Designed with condensation, legibility and clarity in mind, it works across titles, captions and body text in tight layouts, with nine weights, matching italics and broad Latin language support for print and digital use.

name: Kaneda Gothic
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric 'gaspipe' gothic rooted in woodtype-era forms, refined with sharp curves; fits squarely in the geometric sans-serif category with display-weight sensibilities.
summary: A geometric sans built on woodtype-era gaspipe letterforms, sharpened for contemporary headline and logo use.
description: Taking the proportions of woodtype-era gaspipe gothics as its starting point, Kaneda Gothic tightens the curves and sharpens the lines for high-impact display settings. Seven weights with matching italics cover the full range from delicate to heavy, making it workable across headlines, captions and title sequences. The design positions itself between retro-industrial feeling and present-day graphic clarity, without committing entirely to either.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Kaneda Gothic as a new take on the basic gothic typeface, built on geometric gaspipe letterforms associated with the woodtype era but refined with sharp curves and lines for contemporary use. They frame it as sitting in the tension between 1980s–90s futurism and modern realities, industrial progress and emotional needs, and evolving trends against timeless functionality. Recommended uses include logos, movie titles, headlines and captions.

name: Sometype Mono
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a monospaced font family intended for coding and tabular layout, placing it firmly in the mono category with no ambiguity.
summary: An open-source monospaced family from Dharma Type, offered in six styles for coding and tabular work.
description: A monospaced family released under the SIL Open Font License, making it freely available for personal and commercial use. Six styles cover the core needs of coding environments and tabular data layout. Dharma Type's offering in this space is practical and unadorned, suited to developer tooling and data-heavy interfaces.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Sometype Mono as an open source monospaced font family designed for coding and tabular layout, consisting of six styles and licensed under the SIL Open Font License v1.1.

name: Calling Code
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a monospaced typeface for coding and tabular data, with a slightly condensed width and playful details such as curly tails distinguishing it from purely utilitarian coding fonts.
summary: A slightly condensed monospaced font with playful curly tails, designed for coding and tabular layouts across four styles.
description: Built for coding and data display, it offers a slightly condensed width to make better use of screen real estate without sacrificing readability. The four styles — Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic — cover the basics expected of a coding font, while curly tail details give it a modest personality above the generic. Available in both OpenType and TrueType formats, with a free trial of the Regular style via DaFont.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Calling Code as a versatile and stylish monospaced font designed for coding and tabular layouts, noting its slightly condensed width, excellent legibility and character distinction, and playful curly tails. It comes in four styles and is available in OpenType and TrueType formats, with the Regular style available to try for free.

name: Taro
classification_notes: Described explicitly as geometric by the foundry, with emphasis on organic balance and neutral universality typical of humanised geometric sans designs.
summary: A geometric sans in eight weights with italics, designed to balance organic warmth with neutral legibility across Latin-based languages.
description: Dharma Type position Taro around three qualities: organic flow, neutrality and readability, aiming for a geometric sans that feels approachable rather than clinical. Eight weights with matching italics give it range from display all-caps use to extended body text. Broad Latin language support makes it a workmanlike choice for international projects.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Taro as a geometric typeface striking a harmonious balance between natural, organic qualities, neutrality, and legibility. The family includes eight weights with matching italics and supports almost all Latin-based languages, delivering clarity and understated elegance whether set in all-caps for short text or mixed-case for longer passages.

name: Commuters Sans
classification_notes: Described as defined by simple geometry and slightly wide proportions, placing it squarely in the geometric sans tradition, with warm clarity suggesting humanist influence.
summary: A slightly wide geometric sans in eight weights with italics, designed for both body text and display use across Latin-based languages.
description: Eight weights with matching italics give this geometric sans a broad working range, from body copy to titling. The slightly wide proportions and minimalist letterforms sit comfortably in editorial and branding contexts, and the family handles all-caps short lines as well as mixed-case running text. Coverage extends to almost all Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type position Commuters Sans as a daily-use typeface that balances classic and modern sensibilities through simple geometry, slightly wide proportions and warm clarity. The family spans eight weights with matching italics and is suited to editorial, branding and everyday communication, performing equally well in body text and titling.

name: Rigid Square
classification_notes: Defined by octagonal forms and strict 45-degree cuts, this is a geometric sans with neo-humanist modifications to key letterforms for improved legibility at text sizes.
summary: A geometric sans built on octagonal forms and 45-degree cuts, adapted for extended reading through neo-humanist letterforms on key characters.
description: Strict geometry defines the construction, with octagonal shapes and precise 45-degree angles throughout. Dharma Type have tempered this rigidity with clearly distinguishable forms for capital I and lowercase l, and neo-humanist shaping on b, d, g, p and q, making it serviceable at body text sizes in technical and mechanical contexts. Seven weights with matching italics and broad Latin coverage make it functional across both documentation and display work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Rigid Square as governed by a single rule: octagonal shapes and precise 45-degree cuts. Despite its strict geometry, the family has been designed with readability in mind, featuring distinguishable I and l forms and neo-humanist shapes for b, d, g, p and q. It covers seven weights with matching italics and supports nearly all Latin-based languages, positioned as a versatile option for technical documentation and display use alike.

name: Mighty Slab
classification_notes: A heavy, blocky slab-serif designed explicitly for display use at large sizes; the ultra-heavy weight and layered colour font system place it firmly in the display slab category.
summary: A ultra-heavy display slab from Dharma Type built around four layerable styles for colour stacking effects in headlines and logos.
description: Four styles, Mighty Slab One, Shadow, Inline and Texture, stack on top of each other to produce layered colour type effects without specialist software. The super-high x-height and blocky letterforms are tuned for maximum weight at large sizes, covering Adobe Latin 2 and most of Latin 3. Works best in short-burst title settings, poster work and brand wordmarks where sheer mass is the intent.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Mighty Slab as a bold and ultra-heavy slab-serif designed for titles, captions and logos, with strong blocky letterforms built for attention-grabbing headlines and powerful brand statements. It ships as four layerable colour font styles that can be stacked in the same position, with Mighty Slab One usable independently, and includes 531 glyphs covering Mac Roman, Windows 1252 and most of Adobe Latin 3.

name: Bio Sans Soft
classification_notes: Geometrically constructed skeleton with softened, rounded terminals and a large x-height; positioned as a neutral, legible all-purpose sans with humanist friendliness.
summary: A geometrically constructed sans with softened rounded details, designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa for readable, all-purpose text and UI work.
description: Built on a geometric skeleton with subtle rounded terminals, it offers a friendly, neutral appearance suited to body text, UI and editorial contexts. Six weights run from ExtraLight to ExtraBold, each with matching italics, and the large x-height holds up well at small sizes on screen. OpenType features cover fractions, ordinals, superior and inferior figures, and case-sensitive forms, with broad coverage of Latin-based European languages including Afrikaans.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Bio Sans Soft as a super-neutral sans-serif family sharing the same core design philosophy as Bebas Neue, characterised by neutrality, minimalism and legibility. The geometrically constructed letterforms are softened with rounded details for an approachable feel, and the family supports Western, Central and South Eastern European languages alongside a full set of OpenType typographic features.

name: Bio Sans
classification_notes: Built on a geometric skeleton with optical corrections, designed for neutrality and legibility at text sizes. The foundry describes it as super-flat and transparent, placing it squarely in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A neutral geometric sans in six weights with italics, designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa for text use across digital and print media.
description: Built on a geometric skeleton with optical refinements, it shares a design philosophy with Bebas Neue in its pursuit of flatness and transparency, though here applied to text rather than display use. A large x-height aids readability at small sizes on screen, and the family covers Western, Central and South Eastern European languages alongside Afrikaans. OpenType features include proportional figures, fractions, ordinals and case-sensitive forms for professional typographic work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Bio Sans as a super-neutral sans-serif optimised for clarity and versatility in text settings. They note it shares the core philosophy of Bebas Neue, embodying principles of being neutral, natural, minimal, harmless, super-flat and transparent. The family spans six weights with matching italics, supports broad European language coverage, and includes a full suite of OpenType features.

name: Gomme Sans
classification_notes: Described as a wide, squarish sans with bold letterforms and strong visual impact, placing it in the grotesque tradition with a geometric influence suited to both text and display work.
summary: A wide, squarish sans-serif in six weights with matching italics, designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa for both text and display settings.
description: Squarish letterforms and a large x-height give Gomme Sans a sturdy, commanding presence at display sizes while retaining legibility in smaller text on screen. The family runs from ExtraLight to ExtraBold, each with a matching italic, covering a broad tonal range for editorial and brand work. OpenType features include proportional figures, fractions, superior and inferior figures, and case-sensitive forms, with coverage across Western, Central and South Eastern European languages as well as Afrikaans.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Gomme Sans as a wide, masculine sans-serif family tailored for text and display use, with squarish bold letterforms designed for powerful visual impact. Its sophisticated construction and large x-height ensure legibility at small screen sizes, and its comprehensive OpenType feature set makes it a versatile choice for modern typography across a wide range of European languages.

name: Quiet Sans
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a super geometric sans-serif, with crisp and sharp geometric letterforms designed for text and screen use rather than display purposes.
summary: A geometric sans-serif by Ryoichi Tsunekawa in six weights with matching italics, built for text legibility on digital screens.
description: Crisp, sharp letterforms drawn on a geometric skeleton make up this text-oriented family from Dharma Type. Six weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold each come with matching italics, and the family supports Western, Central and South Eastern European languages as well as Afrikaans. Advanced OpenType features including fractions, superior and inferior figures, ordinals and case-sensitive forms add functional depth for body copy and UI work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Quiet Sans as a super geometric sans-serif designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa for text and screen readability, combining crisp geometric letterforms with sophisticated design to ensure legibility at small sizes on digital screens. The family covers six weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold with matching italics, broad European language support, and a comprehensive set of OpenType features.

name: Siro
classification_notes: Described as clean and neutral with a large x-height optimised for text legibility; the simple letterform skeleton and text focus align with humanist sans conventions rather than strict geometric or grotesque models.
summary: A neutral, text-optimised sans-serif in seven weights with matching italics, built around a large x-height for compact and on-screen use.
description: Designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa, Siro is built on a clean letterform skeleton aimed at conveying a natural, unobtrusive impression across print and digital contexts. Its very large x-height is the defining optical choice, improving legibility at small sizes and in tight layouts. The family covers Western, Central and South Eastern European languages as well as Afrikaans, and ships with a thorough OpenType feature set including fractions, superior and inferior figures, ordinals and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Siro as a sans-serif family optimised for text, emphasising clarity and usability across 7 weights from ExtraLight to Heavy, each with matching italics. They highlight its very large x-height for legibility on small screens and in compact layouts, broad European language support including Afrikaans, and a comprehensive OpenType feature set covering proportional figures, fractions, ordinals and case-sensitive forms.

name: Graphie
classification_notes: Built on a geometric foundation with sharp corners, crisp straight lines and a large x-height, placing it firmly in the geometric sans-serif tradition with optical corrections applied for refinement.
summary: A geometric sans-serif by Ryoichi Tsunekawa with a large x-height and sharp geometry across eight weights with matching italics.
description: Sixteen styles span eight weights from Thin to ExtraBold, each paired with an italic, giving solid coverage for both display and body work. Sharp corners and crisp straight lines sit alongside optical corrections, keeping the family clean without becoming mechanical. European language support covers Western, Central and South Eastern European scripts including Afrikaans, and OpenType features include fractions, superior and inferior figures.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Graphie as a modern geometric sans-serif designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa, offering 16 styles across eight weights with italics. They highlight its sharp corners, crisp lines and large x-height as delivering clarity and precision, with a neutral and universal aesthetic suited to branding, editorial and UI/UX. The family supports nearly all European languages and includes advanced OpenType typographic features.

name: Compasse
classification_notes: Described as semi-modular with a neutral, universal character; the controlled geometry and semi-condensed proportions place it in the geometric sans tradition, softened by humanist intuition.
summary: A semi-condensed geometric sans in six weights with italics, built on a semi-modular approach that tempers rigidity with the designer's intuition.
description: Ryoichi Tsunekawa's Compasse covers six weights from Thin to ExtraBold, each with a matching italic, giving twelve styles in total. Its semi-condensed proportions and large x-height support use across titles, headlines and body text without sacrificing legibility at smaller sizes. The semi-modular construction keeps letterforms feeling balanced rather than mechanical, and broad European language coverage including Western, Central and South Eastern European scripts makes it viable for multilingual editorial and corporate work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Compasse as a semi-condensed sans-serif family designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa, comprising twelve styles across six weights with matching italics. They note its semi-modular approach avoids the rigidity of purely modular designs, resulting in balanced and refined letterforms. The family supports a wide range of European languages and includes OpenType features for fractions, superior and inferior figures, and other advanced typographic controls.

name: Pero
classification_notes: A condensed rounded sans-serif with modular construction and soft terminals; sits between geometric and humanist traditions with its contemporary urban character and warm approachability.
summary: A condensed rounded sans-serif by Ryoichi Tsunekawa, built on a modular skeleton with minimised stems and soft terminals across seven weights.
description: The family runs from ExtraLight to ExtraBold with a large x-height to aid legibility at smaller sizes, making it viable for body text as well as display work. Letterforms are constructed on a modular grid, then stripped of unnecessary strokes and finished with rounded terminals, producing a clean but warm result. Language support covers Western, Central and South Eastern European scripts plus Afrikaans, and OpenType features include superior and inferior figures, fractions, numerators and denominators.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Pero as a condensed rounded sans-serif offering seven weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold. They note its modular skeleton and minimised stems, finished with rounded terminals, give it a contemporary urban feel with a warm and approachable tone. The large x-height aids legibility across all sizes, making it suitable for titles, headlines and body text, with broad European language support and full OpenType figure features.

name: Como
classification_notes: Built on a geometric foundation with rounded terminals, Como sits clearly in the geometric sans tradition, softened by its rounded stroke endings rather than strict circular geometry.
summary: A geometric rounded sans in eight weights by Ryoichi Tsunekawa, balancing neutral structure with softened terminals for broad usability.
description: Designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa and published by Dharma Type, Como is a geometric sans-serif whose rounded terminals temper its otherwise neutral architecture. Eight weights from ExtraLight to Heavy give it range across digital interfaces and print materials, while a large x-height supports legibility at smaller sizes. Latin coverage extends across Western, Central and South Eastern European languages plus Afrikaans, with OpenType support for fractions and superior and inferior figures.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Como as a modern rounded sans-serif family of eight weights built on a geometric foundation, with rounded terminals that balance neutrality and warmth. They highlight its large x-height for legibility, its suitability across all media from digital interfaces to print, and its support for Western, Central and South Eastern European languages and Afrikaans, along with OpenType features including superior and inferior figures, fractions, denominators and numerators.

name: Kiro
classification_notes: A minimal condensed sans-serif with semi-modular construction and subtly rounded corners, sitting between geometric and humanist tendencies without committing fully to either.
summary: A condensed, semi-modular sans by Ryoichi Tsunekawa with a large x-height and subtly rounded corners across six weights.
description: Designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa, Kiro is a condensed sans-serif built on a semi-modular structure where unnecessary stems are stripped away and corners carry a slight rounding. The family runs to 12 styles across six weights from Thin to ExtraBold, each with a matching italic. A generous x-height keeps it legible at body sizes, while the narrow proportions make it well suited to headlines and tight editorial layouts.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Kiro as a minimal, condensed sans-serif designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa, offering 12 styles across six weights with matching italics. They highlight its semi-modular structure, large x-height and subtly rounded corners as delivering a balance of contemporary precision and approachable character, suited to editorial and branding work. The family supports Western, Central and South Eastern European languages, plus Afrikaans, and includes OpenType features for superior and inferior figures, fractions and more.

name: Piepie
classification_notes: An ultra-heavy titling face with a large x-height and sharp details, designed exclusively for display and headline use, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: An ultra-heavy display face with a large x-height and sharp detail, built for headlines and posters.
description: Piepie is a single-weight titling typeface with an outsized x-height, minimal ascenders and descenders, and crisp sharp terminals. Its 461-glyph set covers Latin Extended character sets up to Adobe Latin 3, and OpenType alternates are provided for K, R and Y. The weight and geometry make it most at home in poster work and large-scale display settings.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Piepie as an ultra-heavy typeface designed specifically for titling and captions, noting its bold weight and strong presence as suited to headlines, posters and display settings. They highlight its super-mini ascenders and descenders, ultra-large x-height, sharp detail and OpenType alternates for K, R and Y.

name: Gokuri
classification_notes: Described as a logo typeface for the Suntory Gokuri brand, placing it firmly in the display/custom logotype category with a commercial identity purpose.
summary: A custom logotype designed for the Suntory Gokuri brand, produced by Dharma Type.
description: Created by Dharma Type for the Suntory Gokuri beverage brand, this is a purpose-built logotype rather than a retail typeface. The page offers minimal detail beyond the logo mark itself. Its inclusion in Dharma Type's portfolio suggests it as an example of the foundry's custom lettering and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type present Gokuri as a logo design created for Suntory Gokuri, shown as a standalone brand identity project on their site.

name: Bebas Kai
classification_notes: A display typeface designed specifically for headlines and titling, with an all-caps structure and tall proportions typical of condensed display sans-serif lettering.
summary: A free all-caps display typeface from Ryoichi Tsunekawa, intended for headlines, captions and titling work.
description: Part of the Bebas family from Dharma Type, Kai is a free display cut designed for large-scale headline and titling use. It carries the tall, condensed character of the broader Bebas range and is released under the SIL Open Font Licence. Straightforward and no-frills, it suits posters and display contexts where a bold all-caps sans is needed without cost.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Bebas Kai as a free display font for headline, caption and titling use, designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa and released under the SIL Open Font Licence v1.1.

name: Font Aid VII – Here Comes The Sun
classification_notes: A symbolic, illustrative dingbat or display glyph contribution to the Font Aid VII charity project; the imagery is pictorial and decorative rather than typographic in a conventional sense.
subfamilies: Font Aid VII – Here Comes The Sun
summary: A single-glyph charity contribution to Font Aid VII, combining sun and flood-rescue imagery as a symbol of hope.
description: Created by Dharma Type as part of the Font Aid VII collaborative charity project organised by SOTA, this contribution pairs the sun with a floating rescue tube to reference flood disaster relief. All proceeds from the Font Aid font are donated to charitable organisations. The piece is illustrative rather than typographic, functioning as a symbolic pictogram within the collective font.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe their Font Aid VII contribution, titled 'Here Comes The Sun', as a symbolic fusion of the sun as a beacon of hope and a floating tube representing lives saved during flood disasters. The font is sold through vendors listed on the SOTA website, with all proceeds going to charitable organisations supporting those in need.

name: Controller
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric rounded sans-serif; the precise geometric structure with subtle rounding places it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory with humanist softening.
summary: A geometric rounded sans with five weights and obliques, drawing on late 20th-century West Coast techno-organic design aesthetics.
description: Precise geometric construction is tempered by soft rounded terminals, giving the family a clean, approachable character suited to both screen and print. An Extended width variant broadens its range, and an alternate uppercase 'N' via OpenType stylistic sets offers a small measure of typographic flexibility. Well-suited to branding, UI and motion graphics work where a friendly geometric tone is required.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Controller as a geometric rounded sans-serif inspired by the blend of techno and organic design trends from the late 20th-century West Coast. It combines precise geometric structure with subtle rounding to balance the mechanical with a human touch, offering five weights with matching obliques and extended styles for added versatility.

name: Clonoid
classification_notes: Geometric framework with rounded bowls and semi-rounded edges places it firmly in the geometric sans category, with a retro-futurist inflection drawn from 1980s and 1990s arcade game lettering.
summary: A geometric sans drawn from 1980s and 1990s arcade game logos, balancing angular precision with rounded bowls across six weights.
description: Inspired by the lettering of classic arcade game logos, Clonoid pairs a clean geometric structure with softened bowls and semi-rounded edges to temper its mechanical feel. Six weights from ExtraLight to Black come with matching italics, giving the family twelve styles in total. The result sits between retro-futurist display work and more everyday tech-adjacent applications.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Clonoid as a tribute to arcade game logos from the 1980s and 1990s. Its geometric framework gives a futuristic, sci-fi and mechanical quality, while rounded bowls and semi-rounded edges introduce warmth. The family covers six weights with matching italics, totalling twelve styles, and is available in OpenType format.

name: Antique Spencerian
classification_notes: Directly inspired by Spencerian script, a 19th-century copperplate-derived cursive tradition; the hand-distressed texture and ornamented capitals place it firmly in the decorative script category.
summary: A hand-distressed Spencerian script in two styles, drawing on a 19th-century MacKellar, Smiths & Jordan original for formal and decorative use.
description: Two styles make up the family: a Standard cut suited to both text and display work, and an Ornamented Capitals variant with decorative flourishes for headlines, invitations and logos. Each glyph has been individually hand-distressed to produce an antique texture that reads as genuinely aged rather than digitally filtered. The combination of formal Spencerian structure with worn detailing makes it a practical choice for projects that need period character without resorting to generic Victorian pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Antique Spencerian as an antique script inspired by Spencerian script originally released by MacKellar, Smiths & Jordan in the 19th century. The family pairs a classic Standard style for broad text and display use with Ornamented Capitals for decorative applications such as wedding invitations and logos, with each glyph hand-distressed to achieve an authentic antique texture.

name: Griffon
classification_notes: A titling serif with classic early 20th-century proportions, designed explicitly for headline and display use rather than continuous text setting.
summary: A titling serif with five weights and small caps, drawn from early 20th-century letterform conventions for headline and branding use.
description: Griffon is a display serif family built around classic proportions reminiscent of type from the 1900s to 1930s. Five weights from Light to Bold each include small caps, giving designers some typographic range within what is otherwise a purely display-oriented family. Best suited to masthead work, packaging and identities that want a period-influenced gravitas without resorting to outright revival.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Griffon as a titling serif influenced by classic letterforms and inspired by retro typefaces from the early 20th century. Completely redesigned from scratch, the family spans five weights with small caps throughout, aimed at headlines, titles and branding where powerful, elegant letterforms are needed.

name: Revolution Gothic P
classification_notes: A hand-distressed grotesque display face inspired by propaganda poster lettering; the weathered texture and vintage styling push it firmly into display territory rather than functional sans-serif.
summary: A hand-distressed companion to Revolution Gothic, designed to replicate the weathered texture of aged propaganda posters from the 1960s to 1980s.
description: Built on the Revolution Gothic family, itself an extended reworking of PAG Revolucion from Prop-A-Ganda (2008), this version applies careful hand-distressing to each glyph to mimic naturally worn print. Four styles cover Roman, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic. Best suited to poster work, vintage branding and editorial contexts where authentic surface degradation is part of the brief.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Revolution Gothic P as a distressed version of Revolution Gothic, inspired by retro propaganda posters and wall paintings from the 1960s to 1980s. The glyphs have been hand-distressed to replicate naturally aged and weathered print, and the family covers Roman, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic for both print and web use.

name: Revolution Gothic
classification_notes: Heavily stylised uppercase-dominant display type inspired by mid-20th century propaganda poster lettering and wall paintings; the bold, condensed forms and political vernacular roots place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An expanded reworking of PAG Revolucion, drawing on mid-20th century propaganda poster lettering with 10 styles including new weights, slanted cuts and lowercase.
description: Originally released by Prop-A-Ganda in 2008 and inspired by propaganda posters and wall paintings from the 1960s to 1980s, this updated release refines spacing and details throughout. The family now spans 10 styles with newly added weights, matching slanted variants and lowercase characters absent from the original. Best suited to retrospective branding, editorial headlines and poster work where a period-political flavour is appropriate.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Revolution Gothic as an expanded and refined version of PAG Revolucion, originally Prop-A-Ganda's most popular release. Inspired by propaganda posters and wall paintings from the 1960s to 1980s, the updated family adds new weights, slanted styles and lowercase characters, bringing it to 10 styles for modern design use.

name: Yummo
classification_notes: Described explicitly as geometric sans-serif with rounded contours and a slightly condensed proportion; the rounded terminals and minimal glyphs place it firmly in the geometric rounded subcategory.
summary: A five-weight geometric rounded sans with slightly condensed proportions and soft rounded contours aimed at modern, friendly applications.
description: Geometric in structure but softened by rounded terminals, Yummo sits between the precision of a strict geometric sans and the warmth of a more humanist approach. The slightly condensed proportions lend it efficiency on the page without sacrificing openness, and the minimal glyph forms keep it feeling contemporary. Five weights with diacritic support for most European languages make it a practical choice for interface, branding and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Yummo as a geometric yet slightly condensed sans-serif family designed for versatility. They highlight its precisely shaped minimal glyphs for a modern impression, while rounded contours add softness and charm. The family is said to convey human warmth and personality as if the forms were subtly handwritten, and includes five weights with diacritic support covering most European languages.

name: Sheepman
classification_notes: Directly inspired by William Page's 19th-century wood type No.506, Sheepman belongs to the display category with a wood type subcategory, distinguished by its rounded softening of the original polygonal letterforms.
summary: A wood type display family derived from William Page's No.506, with rounded edges softening the original's hard polygonal construction.
description: Sheepman takes William Page's 19th-century No.506 as its starting point and trades angular polygonal forms for rounded edges, lending the letterforms a gentler character than their historical source. Three weights are included, each with a matching slanted style, and OpenType Stylistic Alternates and Swash features provide alternate glyphs for a selection of upper and lowercase characters. Best suited to poster headlines, titles and branding projects where a warm, vintage-inflected display face is needed.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Sheepman as a modern wood type inspired by William Page's No.506, one of the most iconic wooden typefaces of the 19th century. While the original used polygonal shapes, Sheepman introduces rounded edges for a gentler, more natural impression, with every glyph crafted to balance nostalgic charm with contemporary usability.

name: Geom Graphic
classification_notes: A square geometric sans-serif with subtle rounded corners, explicitly compared to Eurostile but more angular; fits squarely in the geometric sans category with display characteristics.
summary: A square geometric sans drawn from the visual language of 1980s Japanese robot animation, with subtle rounding that softens its angular construction.
description: Built around squared geometry with gently rounded corners, this family sits closer to Eurostile territory but pushes further into angular, blocky forms reminiscent of mecha anime aesthetics. Four weights with matching italics give it enough range for logotypes, game interfaces and film titles. The retro-futurist character makes it well-suited to sci-fi themed projects where a cold geometric sans would feel too clinical.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Geom Graphic as a square geometric sans-serif inspired by 1980s Japanese robot animations including Gundam and Ideon. They note it shares visual territory with Eurostile but is more squared and geometric, with subtle rounding that gives it a warm, retro feel. The family ships in four weights with italics and includes extensive language support, targeting futuristic design applications across games, movies and logos.

name: Space Colony
classification_notes: A wide, rounded geometric display face with a retro-futurist character, designed explicitly for sci-fi themed projects, games and animation merchandise rather than text use.
summary: A wide, rounded geometric display family in six weights, drawn with retro robot animation and sci-fi projects in mind.
description: Originally sketched with the side characters of 1980s mecha anime in mind, Space Colony was shelved before being finished in 2011 with its geometric shapes and rounded terminals intact. Six weights make it workable across games, film titles, logos and character merchandise where a futuristic flavour is needed. The soft, wide forms give it a dual character: capable of reading as boldly sci-fi or gently cartoonish depending on context.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Space Colony as a wide, geometric font family that captures a futuristic spirit, originally conceived with retro robot animations such as Gundam and Ideon in mind. Released in 2011 across six weights, it was designed for games, movies, logos and sci-fi projects, though its rounded forms also lend it a soft, cute charm suited to cartoons, animations and character merchandise.

name: House of Cards
classification_notes: A revival of Hamilton's Teniers, a 19th-century wood type design, with display proportions and bold character suited to poster and titling work. Categorised as display due to its heritage in large-scale wood type printing.
summary: A revival of Hamilton's Teniers wood type, offered in two weights with italics and sprayed stroke alternates for period-appropriate poster lettering.
description: Based on Teniers, one of the more popular wood type faces of the 19th century, this revival retains the retro character of the original while modifying the lowercase for a slightly more contemporary feel. Two weights ship with matching italics, and OpenType Stylistic Alternates or Swash features unlock sprayed stroke variants for letters including F, H, P, U and others. Best suited to posters, display titles and branding that wants a convincing vintage register.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe House of Cards as a vintage-inspired typeface based on Hamilton's Teniers, one of the most popular wood type fonts of the 19th century. Lowercase characters have been lightly modified from the original for a more natural and contemporary feel, while retaining the retro charm of classic wood type. The family includes two weights with matching italics and sprayed stroke alternates accessible via OpenType features.

name: Diamond Ring
classification_notes: A decorative display typeface rooted in Art Deco geometry, distinguished by uniform diagonal stroke angles drawn from Japanese cosmetic and poster design of the late 19th to early 20th century.
summary: A geometric display face inspired by Art Deco Japanese packaging and poster design, built around a consistent diagonal stroke angle.
description: All diagonal strokes share an identical angle, producing a rhythmic uniformity that sets this typeface apart from looser Art Deco interpretations. The influence is specifically the Japanese cosmetic and promotional graphics of the late 19th to early 20th century, lending it a precision that feels more structured than period pastiche. The name references the shimmering quality the uniform diagonals create, evoking light caught on a faceted stone.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Diamond Ring as an Art Deco-inspired font drawing influence from Japanese design found in cosmetic packaging and posters from the late 19th to early 20th century. Its defining feature is the use of uniform diagonal strokes that all share the same precise angle, giving the typeface a sense of consistency, rhythm and geometric elegance reminiscent of a diamond ring shimmering during a total solar eclipse.

name: Spoon
classification_notes: The foundry describes a geometric skeleton softened by humanistic outlines with subtle handwritten influence, placing it between geometric and humanist sans-serif traditions.
summary: A geometric sans with humanistic softening across seven weights, suitable for both display and body text use.
description: Spoon pairs a minimal geometric letterform structure with humanistic outlines that lend a subtle, approachable warmth to the design. Seven weights ship with matching italics, and diacritic support covers most European languages throughout. The result sits comfortably across both headline and body text settings where a clean but not cold tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: Dharma Type describe Spoon as a fresh and contemporary sans-serif designed for versatility. Its geometrically based skeleton is softened with humanistic outlines, giving a subtle handwritten influence that makes it clean and legible while remaining natural and friendly. It includes seven weights with matching italics and supports diacritics for most European languages, positioning it as a solution for both display and body text.

name: All Round Gothic
classification_notes: Inspired by Avant Garde Gothic and Futura, this is a geometric sans-serif with circular bowls and structured geometry, softened with organic touches that distinguish it from strict modernist predecessors.
summary: A geometric sans by Ryoichi Tsunekawa that softens the rigour of Futura and Avant Garde Gothic with imperfectly circular bowls and a warmer overall tone.
description: Designed in 2012 with nine weights and matching obliques, it draws on 1920s modernist geometry while introducing subtle organic irregularity through its bowls and reduced stems. The result sits closer to approachable than austere, making it useful across headlines, body text, logos and motion work. It gained wider exposure through its appearance in the Minions film.
